titleEn: The Protein Brewery wins EU approval for Fermotein mycoprotein six years after novel foods filing
titleZh: The Protein Brewery的Fermotein真菌蛋白获欧盟批准，六年新食品申请终落地
summaryZh: 荷兰公司The Protein Brewery的Fermotein真菌蛋白（学名Rhizomucor pusillus菌丝体）获得欧盟新型食品法规批准，成为首个获准在欧盟销售的新型菌丝体成分。该产品已在新加坡获批、在美国获得自我GRAS认定，并正在英国、加拿大、澳大利亚/新西兰推进监管。公司计划2027年供应600吨，2029年产能超2000吨。CEO称非无菌发酵工艺显著降低资本和运营成本。GFI Europe呼吁欧盟加快监管流程。
featuredReason: 虽然该新闻主要涉及食品科技和监管，但其非无菌发酵工艺和菌丝体蛋白规模化生产路径，为智慧畜牧行业在替代蛋白原料（如饲料添加剂）的工业化生产提供了可借鉴的低成本生物制造思路。
contentFull: The Protein Brewery wins EU approval for Fermotein mycoprotein six years after novel foods filing June 17, 2026 | Elaine Watson Alternative protein Biomanufacturing & biologicals EMEA FoodTech Startups & funding Thijs Bosch: 'This sets a historical precedent in food technology.' Image credit: The Protein Brewery The first novel mycelium ingredient authorized for sale in the EU under the Novel Food Regulation should hit the market in Q3, 2026, after the European Commission gave The Protein Brewery’s Fermotein mycoprotein its stamp of approval, six years after it submitted a dossier. The decision follows a positive vote of the Standing Committee on Plants, Animals, Food and Feed in May and a positive scientific opinion published by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) in December. Fermotein—which the EC says can be labeled as Rhizomucor pusillus mycelium—is approved for sale in Singapore and has self-GRAS status in the US, with regulatory dossiers advancing in the UK, Canada and Australia/New Zealand. The Protein Brewery anticipates supplying 600 metric tons of Fermotein in 2027 from its demo-scale factory in Breda in the Netherlands to meet customer commitments in Europe, the US and Singapore, with production capacity increasing to >2,000 tons by 2029. CEO Thijs Bosch told AgFunderNews: “The unit economics improve materially as we scale through our planned capacity build-out. Our production process uses non-sterile fermentation, which structurally lowers capital and operating costs compared to other biotech fermentation approaches, and that cost advantage compounds as volumes grow.” Asked about the naming conventions, he said: “The Latin name is the regulatory designation for the back-of-pack labelling of foods containing Fermotein, not the consumer-facing language. We see our US and Singapore customers using “mycoprotein powder”, “mycelium protein” or “fermented protein” in consumer-facing language. As the mycelium category grows, we expect the consumer language to become mainstream, just as ‘plant-based protein’ did before it.” “This sets a historical precedent in food technology. The EU system has now confirmed that a novel, whole-food mycelium ingredient fits in the existing Novel Food framework, a pathway the wider European food biotech sector has watched closely for years.” Thijs Bosch, CEO, The Protein Brewery GFI Europe: Regulators need to move more quickly Lea Seyfarth, policy manager at nonprofit think tank the Good Food Institute Europe, welcomed the news but said the fact approval had taken six years “shows the need to ensure the regulatory framework keeps pace with European food innovation.” She added: “The EU should prevent unnecessary future delays by boosting the European Food Safety Authority’s capacity and enabling regulators to provide extended scientific advice and detailed guidance to applicants before submission.” Bosch explained: “The duration was largely a function of being first. Fermotein is the first novel mycelium ingredient to successfully go through EFSA, which meant assessment questions were being defined as we went, including two rounds of additional, costly studies requested two and four years after initial submission. “The current funding environment makes a six-year regulatory timeline genuinely difficult for early-stage companies, and one structural improvement we would welcome is for EFSA to have a mandate to discuss study designs scientifically with applicants, which the upcoming EU Biotech Act could enable. The good news for the companies following us is that the precedent is now set, so the next mycelium applicants should have a clearer and faster path.” Beyond alt meat… Quorn—which grows a filamentous fungus called Fusarium venenatum in fermentation tanks to make meat alternatives—has been in a field of one for many years. However, a flurry of startups has recently emerged developing whole biomass products from various microbes, from Nature’s Fynd and Superbrewed Foods to Infinite Roots, Enifer, ENOUGH Foods, The Better Meat Co, MyForest Foods, MOA Foodtech, and Meati Foods. Several other players such as Unibio and Calysta are also growing protein-rich ingredients with microbes using gas fermentation (rather than using sugars as feedstocks) with an initial focus on animal feed and petfood. While most mycoprotein players are focused on alt meat, however, Fermotein is a protein-rich powder that can be used in a wide range of applications, The Protein Brewery CEO Thijs Bosch told AgFunderNews. “We made a conscious choice to produce a powder instead of a wet mycoprotein. We can now easily export Fermotein to markets like the US and Singapore. It’s very neutral tasting and comprises about 50% complete protein, 35% healthy fibers and is full of vitamins and minerals so is mainly used as a nutritional ingredient.” Things in Singapore are “progressing well, with our distribution partner there focused on nutritional powders,” said Bosch. “In the US, we have been shipping commercial volumes through our distribution partner under self-affirmed GRAS status. Our capacity for 2026 is sold out, and our capacity for 2027 is booking out quickly, “The customers buying in for the EU are primarily innovative, fast-moving brands in active nutrition looking for an all-in-one ingredient that delivers complete protein, fiber, and bioactives in a whole-food solution. Ready-to-Mix (RTM) is the most compelling near-term application. We are also seeing strong interest from clean-label bar and dairy alternative formulators, who view Fermotein as a nutritional upgrade to existing concepts rather than a replacement ingredient, which is how plant-based proteins have been positioned.” Share this article Sign up for our weekly newsletter. translationZh:
首个根据欧盟《新型食品法规》获准在欧盟销售的新型菌丝体成分将于2026年第三季度上市，此前欧洲委员会已批准The Protein Brewery的Fermotein真菌蛋白，距其提交档案已有六年。

这一决定是在5月植物、动物、食品和饲料常设委员会投出赞成票、以及欧洲食品安全局（EFSA）于12月发布积极科学意见之后做出的。

Fermotein——欧洲委员会称其可标注为“Rhizomucor pusillus菌丝体”——已在新加坡获批销售，并在美国获得自我GRAS认定，同时其在英国、加拿大和澳大利亚/新西兰的监管档案正在推进中。

The Protein Brewery预计2027年从其位于荷兰布雷达的示范工厂供应600吨Fermotein，以满足欧洲、美国和新加坡的客户承诺，到2029年产能将增至超过2000吨。

首席执行官Thijs Bosch告诉AgFunderNews：“随着我们按计划扩大产能，单位经济性将显著改善。我们的生产工艺采用非无菌发酵，与其他生物技术发酵方法相比，从根本上降低了资本和运营成本，而且随着产量增长，这一成本优势会进一步放大。”

在被问及命名惯例时，他表示：“拉丁名是含有Fermotein食品背面标签的监管指定名称，而非面向消费者的语言。我们看到美国和新加坡的客户在面向消费者的语言中使用‘真菌蛋白粉’、‘菌丝体蛋白’或‘发酵蛋白’。随着菌丝体品类的发展，我们预计消费者语言将像之前的‘植物基蛋白’一样成为主流。”

“这在食品技术领域开创了历史先例。欧盟系统现已确认，一种新型的、全食品菌丝体成分符合现有的新型食品框架，整个欧洲食品生物技术行业多年来一直密切关注这一路径。”

Thijs Bosch，The Protein Brewery首席执行官

**GFI Europe：监管机构需要加快速度**

非营利智库好食品研究所欧洲政策经理Lea Seyfarth对此消息表示欢迎，但表示审批耗时六年“表明需要确保监管框架跟上欧洲食品创新的步伐。”

她补充道：“欧盟应通过增强欧洲食品安全局的能力，并允许监管机构在提交申请前向申请人提供更广泛的科学建议和详细指导，来防止未来不必要的延误。”

Bosch解释说：“时间长短很大程度上是因为我们是第一个。Fermotein是首个新型菌丝体
author: Elaine Watson
